title: Data Types & Type Casting
description: A complete tour of Java's type system — from the 8 primitive types and their exact memory sizes, to wrapper classes, autoboxing, and the rules of implicit widening vs explicit narrowing casts. This chapter destroys the type-confusion bugs that cost marks on every CSE110 exam.

sections.content: Java is a **strongly-typed** language. Every variable must have a declared type, and the compiler enforces type rules at compile time. There are exactly **8 primitive types** — memorize their sizes and ranges. These are stored directly on the **stack** as raw values, not as objects.

sections.label: char is Unsigned!
sections.content: Unlike C/C++, Java's `char` is a **2-byte unsigned integer** representing a Unicode code point (0–65535). It is NOT the same as `byte`. `char c = -1;` is a **compile error** in Java. This distinction is frequently tested.

sections.title: Declaring and Using Primitives

sections.content: Java **automatically** converts a smaller type to a larger type when no data can be lost. This is called **widening** (or implicit promotion). The widening hierarchy is the sequence: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`. Additionally, `char` widens to `int`, `long`, `float`, or `double`.

sections.title: Widening in Action

sections.label: The byte + byte = int Trap!
sections.content: Adding two `byte` values does NOT give a `byte`. Java **promotes** `byte`, `short`, and `char` to `int` before performing ANY arithmetic. So `byte x=10; byte y=20; byte z = x+y;` is a **compile error** — `x+y` is an `int`. You must cast: `byte z = (byte)(x + y);`.

Converting from a **larger type to a smaller** type risks data loss and is therefore **never automatic** — you must write an explicit cast using the `(type)` syntax. The compiler trusts your judgment, but if the value doesn't fit, it truncates or wraps around silently (no exception).

sections.title: Narrowing Cast Mechanics

sections.label: Narrowing Overflow Formula
sections.content: For `byte` overflow: if the value is in range [128, 255], result = value - 256. For `(byte) 200`: 200 - 256 = **-56**. For `(byte) 256` = 256 - 256 = **0**. This formula covers the most common exam questions.

Every primitive type has a corresponding **Wrapper Class** in `java.lang`. Wrappers are objects — they live on the **heap**, can hold `null`, and have utility methods. They are required when working with Java Collections (which only store objects).

sections.title: Autoboxing and Unboxing

sections.label: Integer Cache Trap (== vs .equals())
sections.content: Java caches `Integer` objects for values **-128 to 127**. For these values, `Integer a = 100; Integer b = 100; a == b` is `true` because they point to the **same cached object**. For values outside this range (e.g., 200), `a == b` is `false` even with equal values. **Always use `.equals()` to compare wrapper objects.**
Java applies **automatic type promotion rules** in expressions, even before any explicit cast. Understanding these rules is essential for predicting output of mixed-type expressions.

items: If either operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`.
Otherwise, if either operand is `float`, the other is promoted to `float`.
Otherwise, if either operand is `long`, the other is promoted to `long`.
Otherwise, **both operands are promoted to `int`** (even if they are `byte`, `short`, or `char`).

sections.title: Type Promotion in Mixed Expressions

sections.content: These exercises target the exact type-system questions that appear in CSE110 exams. Work through each before revealing the answer.

sections.title: Exercise 1: Narrowing Overflow Chain
sections.explanation: Line 1: 257 % 256 = 1 (byte wraps every 256). Line 2: `(int) 3.9999` truncates to 3 (never rounds). Line 3: 300 - 256 = 44. Line 4: -130 + 256 = 126 (negative overflow adds 256).

sections.title: Exercise 2: byte Arithmetic Promotion Trap
sections.explanation: `a + b` promotes both to `int` → 200. Storing in `int c` is fine. `(byte) 200` = 200-256 = -56. For `e += 100`: compound operators like `+=` include an implicit narrow cast, so `e = (byte)(e + 100)` = `(byte)(50 + 100)` = `(byte) 150` = 150 - 256 = **-106**. This compound-assignment implicit cast is a very common exam trick.

sections.title: Exercise 3: Autoboxing == Reference Trap
sections.explanation: JVM caches Integer objects for values -128 to 127. So `127` boxes to the SAME cached object — `x == y` compares references and finds them equal (true). But `128` is outside the cache range, so `p` and `q` are two DIFFERENT Integer objects on the heap — `p == q` is false (different references). `.equals()` always compares by VALUE, so both `.equals()` calls are true.

sections.title: Exercise 4: Mixed Arithmetic Promotion
sections.explanation: A: int+long → long = 15. B: long+float → float = 12.5. C: int/int → int = 2 (truncated). D: int/double → double = 2.5. E: TRICKY! `i * 1000000000` computes `5 * 1000000000` as **int*int first** (= 5,000,000,000 which overflows int → -1294967296), THEN stores the overflowed value into `long`. Fix: `(long) i * 1000000000` or `i * 1000000000L`.

sections.title: Exercise 5: Three Type Errors in One Class
sections.error: Four compile-time errors: Line 3: `10000000000` exceeds `int` literal range — needs `L` suffix: `10000000000L`. Line 4: `3.14159265358` is a `double` literal — needs `f` suffix: `3.14159265358f`. Line 5: `200` is out of `byte` range (-128 to 127) — needs cast: `(byte) 200`. Line 6: `char` cannot be negative — needs cast: `(char) -1` or use `'\uFFFF'`. Line 9: `count + 1.0` is `double`, cannot assign to `int` — needs cast or use `1` instead of `1.0`.
sections.explanation: These are the classic 'wrong literal type' and 'narrowing without cast' compile errors that Java beginners encounter constantly. The rules: long literals need `L`, float literals need `f`, narrowing always needs explicit `(type)` cast. Java's type strictness prevents these bugs from becoming runtime surprises.

sections.title: Exercise 6: char Casting & Arithmetic (Tricky!)
sections.answer:
a
97
A
97
A B C D E
sections.explanation: `'a'` prints as character `a`. `'a' + 0` → char+int → int → prints 97 (ASCII). `(char)('a' - 32)` → `(char)(97-32)` = `(char)65` = `'A'`. `(int) c` explicitly widens char to int → 97. The for loop iterates char from 'A'(65) to 'E'(69), incrementing each time — prints A B C D E with trailing space.

items: **Narrowing** (explicit cast required): going against the widening chain — can lose data.
**`byte`/`short`/`char` arithmetic** always promotes to `int` first — result is never `byte`/`short`/`char`.
**Long literal**: always use `L` suffix for values outside int range: `10_000_000_000L`.
**Float literal**: always use `f` suffix: `3.14f`. Without it, `3.14` is a `double`.
**Truncation vs rounding**: `(int) 9.9 = 9`, not 10. Java always **truncates** toward zero.
**byte overflow formula**: value > 127 → result = value - 256. e.g. `(byte) 200 = -56`.
**Wrapper `==`**: only reliable for cached range (-128 to 127). Always use `.equals()` for value comparison.
**Null unboxing**: `Integer n = null; int x = n;` throws `NullPointerException` at runtime.
**Compound operators** (`+=`, `-=`, `*=`) include an **implicit narrow cast** — can silently overflow.
